Reflection: In today's class, we covered the concept of Just Culture and how it shapes the way organizations respond to medication errors. We reviewed the three behaviors in the Just Culture model: human error, which calls for consoling the person involved; at-risk behavior, which calls for coaching; and reckless behavior, which calls for discipline. We also discussed how a traditional blame-based culture drives near misses underground and prevents healthcare teams from learning about the system weaknesses that those near misses reveal. Learning about Just Culture is essential for my development as a pharmacist because it challenged my assumption that every medication error should be treated the same way. I used to believe that any error meant someone was careless, but I now see that treating a harried technician who made an honest slip the same as someone who deliberately bypassed a verification step is both unjust and bad for patient safety. If the staff fears retaliation, errors go unreported, which means the underlying system problems stay invisible. At my internship, I will commit to filing a formal near miss report through our incident reporting system any time I catch an error before it reaches the patient, even when it feels minor. I will also ask my preceptor during our weekly check-in to walk through one recent reported event with me so that I can learn how our site distinguishes between human error, at-risk behavior, and reckless behavior in practice.

Today we covered Just Culture and how healthcare organizations respond to medication errors depending on the behavior involved. We went over the three categories of human error, at-risk behavior, and reckless behavior, and how each one calls for a different response (consoling, coaching, or discipline). We also discussed how blame-based cultures discourage reporting of near misses, while Just Culture encourages staff to speak up. This topic is important to me because I used to assume all medication errors deserved the same consequence. Realizing that intent and system context matter has changed how I think about accountability in the pharmacy. It also made me reflect on how many near misses probably go unreported because staff are afraid of being blamed, and how that fear ends up hurting future patients by keeping broken systems in place. In my internship, I want to be more open when I make mistakes or see them happen.

In class we talked about how workplaces handle medication errors differently. This topic is important because patient safety is a priority in pharmacy and we need to know how to respond when things go wrong. I thought it was a helpful discussion and I will think about it more as I get into my rotations.

Today's session focused on Root Cause Analysis (RCA) as a structured method for investigating medication errors and adverse events. We walked through a case involving an insulin overdose and used the Five Whys technique to move past the initial human error down to the system-level contributors, including a look-alike vial design, inadequate labeling on the floor stock, and a nursing workflow that allowed the barcode scanner to be bypassed. We also discussed how a good RCA focuses on what happened and why, not on who was responsible, and how it should produce system-level action items rather than individual blame. Learning about RCA is essential to my growth because I previously thought of error investigations as fact-finding missions aimed at identifying who was at fault. This session reshaped my understanding by showing that stopping at 'the nurse drew up the wrong dose' prevents meaningful improvement, because the real fix lies upstream in packaging, storage, and workflow design. It also reinforced my belief that pharmacists have a unique responsibility to advocate for system changes, since we see the medication use process end-to-end from prescribing to administration. At my internship, I will ask my preceptor whether I can sit in on any RCA or M&M conferences that happen during my rotation, and if I catch a near miss at the counter I will document the contributing system factors such as storage layout, look-alike packaging, and interruption patterns, in addition to noting what the individual did.

In class, we learned about Root Cause Analysis and how it is used to investigate medical errors. We also talked about the Five Whys technique for identifying why something happened. This learning matters because if we don't understand why errors happen, they will continue and patients will be harmed. Understanding this process helps pharmacists know what to do after an event. In the future, I will try to be more thoughtful about why errors happen and not just who made them.

Today we reviewed Root Cause Analysis and the Five Whys technique, which is a way of asking why something happened several times in a row until you get to the underlying reason. We also went over a case where an error happened and traced it back to system issues like labeling and workflow. This is important for pharmacists because we need to prevent mistakes and make sure patients get the right care. I will keep this in mind as I move through the program.

In today's introduction to patient safety, we reviewed the key taxonomy used to classify safety events: near misses (errors that are caught before reaching the patient), medication errors that reach the patient without causing harm, adverse drug events (injury from a medication, whether preventable or not), and sentinel events (unexpected events that result in death or serious physical or psychological harm). We also discussed The Joint Commission's requirement that sentinel events trigger a formal review, and we mapped a sterile compounding case onto this taxonomy to see how the same underlying failure can fall into different categories depending on whether and how it reaches the patient. Learning this taxonomy is important for my growth because I had previously been using 'medication error' as a catch-all. Having precise language matters both for accurate documentation and for picking the right improvement response. It also challenged my assumption that near misses are 'not a big deal' because no patient was harmed. Near misses are actually the highest-volume, lowest-cost signal we have about system weaknesses, and they deserve the same scrutiny as events that caused harm. During my upcoming hospital rotation, I will ask my preceptor to walk me through how events are categorized in the hospital's reporting platform, and I will use the correct terminology in my own documentation instead of defaulting to 'error.' When I catch a near miss at work, I will file it through the same reporting pathway I would use for a harm event.

Today we talked about the different terms used to describe safety events in healthcare, including near misses, errors, and adverse events. We also touched on how some events are considered more serious and get reported and reviewed more formally. This learning is important because I realized I have been using the word 'error' for everything, and that imprecision can affect how a problem is investigated and ultimately fixed. It also made me reflect on the value of near misses. At the pharmacy where I work, near misses happen often and most of them get mentioned to a coworker and then forgotten. I now see that each one of those is a warning sign that we are ignoring, which is humbling. Going forward, I want to pay more attention to safety events in my workplace.

Today's class went over different safety terms used in healthcare. Patient safety is important because it affects patients and pharmacists have to learn about it. I am not yet sure how this will look in my future job but I will learn more as the class continues.

This session introduced human factors engineering and how cognitive errors contribute to medication safety events. We reviewed the main categories of cognitive failures, including slips (carrying out an intended action incorrectly, such as grabbing the wrong vial off the shelf), lapses (forgetting a step in a procedure), and mistakes (choosing the wrong course of action due to faulty reasoning or incomplete information). We also examined how confirmation bias, fatigue, interruptions, and high workload amplify these cognitive vulnerabilities, and how well-designed systems anticipate human error through forcing functions, redundancies, and standardization. A key takeaway was that human error is predictable and patterned, not a character flaw. This learning is foundational for my development as a pharmacist because it reframes errors as predictable responses to system conditions rather than moral failings. I reflected on times when I have almost made a dispensing error during busy shifts at my internship, and I realized that labeling myself 'careless' in those moments was both inaccurate and unhelpful. Recognizing workload, interruptions, and fatigue as measurable risk factors empowers me to advocate for workflow changes instead of relying on willpower to avoid mistakes. At my internship, I will start keeping a simple log of the situations in which I almost made an error (for example, when an interruption occurs mid-verification, or when I am the sole pharmacist on a holiday shift) so that I can bring concrete data to a conversation with my manager about workflow adjustments. I will also practice teach-back with patients at counseling so that my final cognitive check on a prescription happens in dialogue with the patient rather than alone in my head.

Today we covered human factors in patient safety and how cognitive errors lead to medication mistakes. I learned the difference between slips (such as grabbing the wrong medication off the shelf), lapses (forgetting a step in the dispensing process), and mistakes (making the wrong decision because of faulty reasoning), and how fatigue, interruptions, and high workload make all three more likely. We also discussed how systems should include forcing functions and redundancies to catch these errors before they reach patients. This topic matters because medication errors are a major contributor to patient harm, and pharmacists play a central role in preventing them. Understanding the types of human error helps us respond better when they occur. At my internship, I will pay closer attention to the conditions that put me at higher risk for cognitive errors, such as working through lunch or being interrupted mid-verification, and when I am interrupted during a verification I will restart that task from the beginning rather than picking up where I left off. I will also ask my preceptor what forcing functions are built into our dispensing workflow so I understand which backup catches I am actually relying on.

Today we discussed how people can make mistakes and why cognitive errors happen in pharmacy. We learned about slips, lapses, and mistakes, and how workload and interruptions can contribute to errors. Patient safety is important in healthcare. I will remember this material in the future.

Today's class introduced James Reason's Swiss Cheese Model as the foundational systems-thinking framework for patient safety. I learned that the model represents an organization's defenses as successive slices of cheese, each with holes (latent and active failures), and that a harm event occurs only when the holes momentarily align. The key insight is that no single slice (prescriber, pharmacist, nurse, technology) can be relied on to stop every error; instead, safety comes from layering imperfect defenses so that the holes rarely line up. We also reviewed Reason's distinction between active failures (unsafe acts at the sharp end — e.g., grabbing the wrong vial) and latent conditions (organizational decisions whose consequences lie dormant — understaffing, ambiguous policies, poor lighting). The class emphasized that addressing only active failures ("the technician was distracted") leaves the latent conditions that produced the distraction unchanged.

This shifted my mental model of safety in an important way. I had been instinctively treating individual errors as individual problems, especially in stories about medication errors that named the person involved. The Swiss Cheese Model reframes the same event as a system that allowed the error to reach the patient — and reframes the goal of safety work as adding or repairing defensive layers, not punishing individuals at the sharp end.

I will apply this in my rotations by (1) committing, when I encounter a near miss, to ask "what other defenses should have caught this?" before asking "who made the mistake?"; (2) asking my preceptor to share one institutional RCA with me and explicitly map the Swiss Cheese layers and their holes; and (3) carrying the model into my own dispensing practice — I will think of my verification step as one slice of cheese among many, not the last line of defense, so I am realistic about the system around me.

Today we learned about the Swiss Cheese Model of patient safety. The idea is that errors get through when the holes in different layers of defense line up. The model distinguishes between active failures and latent conditions. This is important because it shows that errors are usually system problems, not just individual mistakes. I will think about systems and not just individuals when I encounter errors at my rotations.
The Swiss Cheese Model is about how errors happen. There are holes in the system. This is part of patient safety.

This session covered "speak-up culture" and TeamSTEPPS as concrete tools for addressing one of the most stubborn problems in patient safety — the failure of team members to voice safety concerns to a more senior or more confident colleague. I learned the TeamSTEPPS techniques specifically designed to lower the cost of speaking up: CUS ("I'm Concerned, I'm Uncomfortable, this is a Safety issue"), the two-challenge rule (if your concern is dismissed once, you must voice it a second time before deferring), and DESC scripts for delivering structured feedback after a critical event. We also reviewed data showing that even experienced clinicians frequently fail to speak up when they see a senior colleague about to make an error — and that the gap is not knowledge or motivation but the absence of pre-rehearsed language to use under pressure.

This mattered to me personally because, as a student about to enter rotations where I will frequently be the most junior person in the room, I had been vaguely worried about "how to push back" without a concrete script. CUS and the two-challenge rule give me both permission and language. The class also made clear that an institution's speak-up culture is not built by training students alone — it requires senior clinicians to respond well when they are challenged, which I will now be alert to during rotations.

I plan to apply this by (1) memorizing the CUS script verbatim so I can use it under pressure rather than having to invent language in the moment; (2) asking my preceptor whether the institution has formal TeamSTEPPS training and if I can attend a session; and (3) when I observe a senior clinician respond well to a challenge from a junior team member, deliberately noting what they did so I can model that behavior when I am the senior person someday.

Today's session covered speak-up culture and TeamSTEPPS tools that help team members raise safety concerns. We learned about CUS ("I am concerned, I am uncomfortable, it's a safety issue") and the two-challenge rule for raising a concern more than once. This is important because team communication is a big part of patient safety. I will try to speak up when I see something unsafe at my rotation.
We talked about speaking up when there is a safety problem. There are tools for this. I will speak up if I need to.

Today's class focused on disclosure and apology after a harmful medical error — the practice of telling patients and families honestly what happened, why it happened, what the institution will do about it, and expressing genuine regret. I learned that the evidence-based components of effective disclosure are (1) timeliness (within 24–48 hours of identification), (2) involvement of the clinician most directly involved alongside an institutional representative, (3) clear factual description without speculation, (4) explicit apology, and (5) follow-up communication as the investigation progresses. We also reviewed the malpractice data — programs like the University of Michigan disclosure program have shown that early honest disclosure paradoxically reduces litigation, total claims costs, and time to resolution, in addition to its ethical importance.

What this learning shifted for me was the assumption that disclosure and legal self-protection are inevitably in tension. The data suggest the opposite. The ethical case for disclosure is straightforward; what surprised me was that the institutional case is also strong. I also realized that disclosure is a skill that requires practice — saying "I'm sorry" at the right moment in the right way is not innate, and clinicians who are not trained tend to under-disclose, speculate, or apologize defensively.

I will apply this by (1) asking my preceptor if the institution has a disclosure protocol and whether I can review one redacted disclosure summary to study the language and structure; (2) practicing a brief disclosure script with my preceptor in a low-stakes simulated scenario before I am ever in a real one; and (3) committing, if I am ever directly involved in an error that reaches a patient during my career, to participate in the disclosure rather than allow the institution to handle it alone — that is part of what I owe the patient.

Today we discussed disclosure of medical errors to patients and families. The key elements are being timely, factual, and offering an apology. We learned that early honest disclosure can actually reduce litigation. This is important because patients deserve to know what happened. I think disclosure is the right thing to do.
When errors happen, you have to tell the patient. This is the right thing to do. I think this is important.

This session contextualized everything we have learned in the patient safety course by walking through the Institute of Medicine's 1999 report "To Err Is Human" and the field-defining shift it produced. I learned that the report estimated 44,000–98,000 preventable deaths per year in U.S. hospitals from medical errors and reframed safety as an emergent property of healthcare systems rather than the result of individual diligence. We reviewed the report's main recommendations — establish a national focus on safety, identify and learn from errors through reporting systems, raise standards through oversight bodies, and implement safety systems at the point of care — and tracked which have made progress (e.g., CMS-mandated reporting, accreditation standards) and which remain unfinished (e.g., interoperable error reporting, consistent disclosure practices).

This learning mattered because it gave me a coherent intellectual frame for the rest of the course. Without the IOM report's premise — that errors are produced by systems, not bad apples — none of the specific tools we have studied (RCA, Just Culture, Swiss Cheese, FMEA) make sense. It also clarified for me why patient safety is sometimes politically contested even within healthcare: "systems thinking" is sometimes resisted as deflecting responsibility from individuals, and being aware of that history will help me communicate about safety work more thoughtfully on rotations.

I will apply this by (1) reading the original IOM report in full before my first inpatient rotation; (2) committing, when I encounter resistance to systems-based safety thinking on rotation, to explain the underlying premise calmly rather than assume bad faith; and (3) using "To Err Is Human" as my internal frame whenever I am tempted to attribute a near miss to a single individual — first asking what about the system made the error possible.

Today's class covered "To Err Is Human," the IOM report from 1999 that established patient safety as a national issue. The report estimated tens of thousands of deaths per year from preventable errors and recommended treating safety as a system property. This is important because it changed how healthcare thinks about errors. I will keep this in mind during my rotations.
There was a famous report about patient safety. It said many people die from errors. Patient safety is important.
